title: A Worked Example: CSV Parser with SKIP-BAD-ROW

sections.markdown:
# A Worked Example: CSV Parser with SKIP-BAD-ROW

Time to tie it all together. We'll write a tiny CSV-like parser that takes a list of row strings, splits each on commas, and parses each field as an integer. Malformed rows will signal a `bad-row` error, and we'll offer two restarts: `skip-row` (drop this row) and `use-row` (substitute a value).

The payoff: the *same* parser function supports strict mode, tolerant mode, and substitution mode — determined entirely by the handler the caller installs.

```lisp
(define-condition bad-row (error)
  ((line :initarg :line :reader bad-row-line)
   (cause :initarg :cause :reader bad-row-cause)))

(defun parse-row (line)
  (mapcar #'parse-integer
          (loop for start = 0 then (1+ comma)
                for comma = (position #\, line :start start)
                collect (subseq line start comma)
                while comma)))

(defun parse-rows (lines)
  (loop for line in lines
        for row = (restart-case
                      (handler-case (parse-row line)
                        (error (c)
                          (error 'bad-row :line line :cause c)))
                    (skip-row () nil)
                    (use-row  (v) v))
        when row collect row))
```

Called with no handler, a bad row signals `bad-row` and the debugger appears. Called under a handler that invokes `skip-row`, bad rows are quietly dropped. Called under a handler that invokes `use-row` with `'(0 0 0)`, bad rows become zeros. The parser itself is unchanged across all three uses.

sections.code: (progn (define-condition bad-row (error) ((line :initarg :line :reader bad-row-line))) (defun parse-row (line) (mapcar #'parse-integer (loop for start = 0 then (1+ comma) for comma = (position #\, line :start start) collect (subseq line start comma) while comma))) (parse-row "1,2,3"))
sections.explain: The splitter plus PARSE-INTEGER on each field. Uses LOOP's WHILE clause to stop when no more commas are found.

sections.code: (progn (define-condition bad-row (error) ((line :initarg :line :reader bad-row-line))) (defun parse-row (line) (mapcar #'parse-integer (loop for start = 0 then (1+ comma) for comma = (position #\, line :start start) collect (subseq line start comma) while comma))) (defun parse-rows (lines) (loop for line in lines for row = (restart-case (handler-case (parse-row line) (error () (error 'bad-row :line line))) (skip-row () nil) (use-row (v) v)) when row collect row)) (handler-bind ((bad-row (lambda (c) (declare (ignore c)) (invoke-restart 'skip-row)))) (parse-rows '("1,2" "oops" "3,4"))))
sections.explain: The bad row 'oops' is dropped by the SKIP-ROW restart; the two good rows parse normally.

sections.code: (progn (define-condition bad-row (error) ((line :initarg :line :reader bad-row-line))) (defun parse-row (line) (mapcar #'parse-integer (loop for start = 0 then (1+ comma) for comma = (position #\, line :start start) collect (subseq line start comma) while comma))) (defun parse-rows (lines) (loop for line in lines for row = (restart-case (handler-case (parse-row line) (error () (error 'bad-row :line line))) (skip-row () nil) (use-row (v) v)) when row collect row)) (handler-bind ((bad-row (lambda (c) (declare (ignore c)) (invoke-restart 'use-row '(0 0))))) (parse-rows '("1,2" "oops" "3,4"))))
sections.explain: Same parser, different handler: the bad row is replaced by (0 0). The parser code didn't change.

sections.prompt: Using the same framework, define COUNT-BAD (LINES) that returns the number of rows that WOULD have signaled BAD-ROW if they weren't skipped. Use HANDLER-BIND to count and invoke SKIP-ROW on each. Assume PARSE-ROW and PARSE-ROWS and BAD-ROW are already defined as in the examples above; you only write COUNT-BAD.
sections.starter:
(define-condition bad-row (error)
  ((line :initarg :line :reader bad-row-line)))

(defun parse-row (line)
  (mapcar #'parse-integer
          (loop for start = 0 then (1+ comma)
                for comma = (position #\, line :start start)
                collect (subseq line start comma)
                while comma)))

(defun parse-rows (lines)
  (loop for line in lines
        for row = (restart-case
                      (handler-case (parse-row line)
                        (error () (error 'bad-row :line line)))
                    (skip-row () nil)
                    (use-row  (v) v))
        when row collect row))

(defun count-bad (lines)
  )
